core_intuitions: The KL-regularized RLHF objective has a closed-form optimum: pi* is the reference policy TILTED by the exponentiated reward and renormalized (a Gibbs/Boltzmann distribution), with beta as the temperature. The only obstacle is the intractable partition function Z(x).

The reward model does not vanish by magic: you invert the optimum to write the reward as beta*log(pi/pi_ref) + beta*logZ(x) (the implicit reward), plug it into Bradley-Terry which depends only on the reward DIFFERENCE, and the identical beta*logZ(x) terms cancel. That single cancellation is the whole trick.

The DPO loss -log sigma(Delta) has a contrastive gradient weighted by sigma(-Delta): it pushes chosen up and rejected down, hard on mis-ranked pairs and near-zero on solved ones (an automatic curriculum). [served by dpo-margin-gradient-regimes]
DPO is offline/off-policy and only re-ranks data it already has, whereas PPO regenerates and explores with a live reward model. [served by dpo-vs-ppo]

The degeneration footgun: the loss only constrains the DIFFERENCE Delta, so reward accuracy and margin can climb while BOTH absolute log-probs fall and mass leaks to unseen/degenerate outputs the loss never sees.

The variant zoo is a small set of orthogonal choices: which reward you read off the policy, what loss shape contrasts winner vs loser, whether you keep a reference model, and whether you need paired data.

existing.name: dpo-margin-gradient-regimes
existing.issue: Renders clearly in both themes and nails the self-curriculum intuition (Delta feeds loss + gradient-weight curves, three regimes along a mis-ranked->solved axis). Minor: the loss curve has a faint piecewise kink near its top-left that could be smoothed, but it does not impair reading.
existing.improvement: Optional: smooth the small kink in the -log sigma(Delta) curve near the y-axis so it reads as one continuous convex curve.

existing.name: dpo-vs-ppo
existing.issue: Clean, legible in both themes; the PPO 4-component online loop vs the DPO single straight-through pass is exactly the contrast the debate section needs. Dark theme is slightly busier but every label stays readable.
existing.improvement: None required. If revisited, add a tiny 'iterative/online DPO' hook arrow looping DPO's aligned output back to fresh pair generation, mirroring the closing synthesis paragraph.

new_figures.name: dpo-reward-model-cancellation
new_figures.concept: The algebraic chain from the KL-RLHF optimum to the DPO loss, showing exactly where the reward model goes and where beta*logZ(x) cancels.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's headline result and its most-asked interview question ('where does the reward model go, and what assumption makes it disappear?'). The derivation is spread across four equation blocks in prose; a single flow that makes the cancellation VISIBLE turns the central 'aha' from something you re-derive into something you see. Nothing in the chapter visualizes this.
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right (or top-to-bottom) derivation pipeline in 4 linked stages, each a labeled panel connected by arrows, with the KEY cancellation dramatized. STAGE 1 'Closed-form optimum': pi*(y|x) = (1/Z(x)) * pi_ref(y|x) * exp(r(x,y)/beta), annotate Z(x) with a small red-flag/warning glyph and the caption 'sums over ALL responses -- intractable'. STAGE 2 'Invert for the reward': r(x,y) = beta*log(pi/pi_ref) + beta*logZ(x); label the first term 'implicit reward = beta * log-ratio (two forward passes)' and the second term 'still intractable... for now'. STAGE 3 'Bradley-Terry needs only the DIFFERENCE': show P(y_w > y_l) = sigma(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l)); write the difference expanded as [beta*log-ratio(w) + beta*logZ(x)] - [beta*log-ratio(l) + beta*logZ(x)], and DRAW A STRIKE-THROUGH across both beta*logZ(x) terms with a callout 'same for winner and loser -> cancels (depends only on x)'. This cancellation is the visual climax -- make it the largest, most central element. STAGE 4 'DPO loss': L = -log sigma(Delta), Delta = beta*[log-ratio(w) - log-ratio(l)], tagged 'no reward model, no RL, no Z(x)'. Add a thin side-rail badge that starts as a 'reward model r_phi' box in stage 1-2 and visibly transforms/collapses into 'reparameterized INTO the policy' by stage 4 -- reinforcing 'the reward model didn't vanish, it was folded into pi_theta'. Theme-safe colors only: use a single accent for the surviving policy terms and a muted/struck treatment for the cancelled Z terms; no hardcoded brand colors. All math must fit inside the viewBox with clear reading order; no overlapping tokens. Small illustrative symbols only, invent no numbers.

new_figures.name: dpo-boltzmann-tilt
new_figures.concept: The optimal aligned policy is the reference distribution reweighted by exp(reward/beta) and renormalized, with beta acting as a temperature that controls how aggressively.
new_figures.rationale: The 'key move' section states pi* is a Gibbs/Boltzmann tilt of the reference, but readers meet it only as an equation. A picture of 'reference bars -> multiply by exp(r/beta) -> renormalize -> tilted optimum', plus a small beta knob, makes the closed-form solution intuitive and grounds why beta is a trust-region temperature. This complements (does not duplicate) the cancellation figure: that one is the algebra, this one is the SHAPE of the answer.
new_figures.placement: ### The key move: this has a closed-form solution
new_figures.brief: A three-step transformation of a small discrete distribution over a handful of candidate responses (say 5-6 bars labeled y1..y6), shown as three bar-chart panels connected by arrows. PANEL 1 'Reference pi_ref': a fairly flat/broad bar distribution over the responses. PANEL 2 'Tilt by exp(r/beta)': overlay a per-response reward as small up/down markers (some responses high-reward, some low, illustrative only -- use relative heights, NO numeric reward values), and show the multiplier exp(r/beta) reweighting each bar; caption 'multiply each response by its exponentiated reward'. PANEL 3 'Renormalize -> pi*': the resulting peaked distribution that concentrates mass on high-reward responses while still resembling the reference (mass does not fully collapse to one bar), captioned 'pi* proportional to pi_ref * exp(r/beta), then divide by Z(x)'. Add a small BETA TEMPERATURE control beneath panel 3 (a slider/dial glyph) with two ghosted mini-distributions: 'small beta -> sharp, drifts far from ref' (very peaked) and 'large beta -> gentle, stays near ref' (nearly flat like panel 1). Include a tiny label 'Z(x) = sum over ALL responses (why we can't just compute this)' hanging off the renormalize arrow to foreshadow the intractability that motivates DPO. Theme-safe: one accent for the tilted/high-reward bars, neutral for reference bars; must render in both light and dark. Everything inside the viewBox, bars and labels legible, no overlaps. Illustrative integer bar heights only.

new_figures.name: dpo-degeneration-footgun
new_figures.concept: Reward accuracy and margin can rise while BOTH chosen and rejected absolute log-probs fall, because the loss only constrains the difference -- probability mass leaks onto unseen outputs.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's second dedicated Interview Corner and a Key Takeaway ('watch ABSOLUTE chosen log-prob, not just reward accuracy'). It is a genuine footgun that a single well-designed static frame makes obvious: the margin looks healthy while the floor drops out. No existing figure shows it, and it directly motivates KTO/IPO/CPO/ORPO anchors and raising beta.
new_figures.placement: !!! interview "Interview Corner" (both chosen and rejected log-probabilities falling)
new_figures.brief: A two-part diagnostic figure contrasting 'what the usual metric shows' vs 'what is actually happening'. LEFT/TOP -- 'Looks healthy': a simple rising line for reward accuracy climbing past a 0.5 baseline toward ~0.7, and a rising 'reward margin (r_w - r_l)' line; a green check labeled 'margin improving'. RIGHT/BOTTOM -- 'The trap': a shared vertical axis 'absolute sequence log-prob' with TWO lines over training steps, chosen log-prob and rejected log-prob, BOTH sloping downward, but rejected falling FASTER so the GAP between them (shade this gap and label it 'Delta = the only thing the loss constrains') still widens. Add a callout arrow 'margin grows because rejected drops faster -- not because chosen improves'. Beneath, a small probability-mass schematic: a bar for pi_theta split into 'chosen (shrinking)', 'rejected (shrinking fast)', and a growing third bucket 'UNSEEN / degenerate outputs -- never in the data, never penalized', with an arrow showing mass leaking from the first two into the third. Footer strip of the four fixes as compact chips: 'raise beta (tighter KL leash)', 'add SFT/NLL anchor on chosen (CPO/ORPO)', 'switch to KTO/IPO (absolute anchor)', 'check rejected data quality'. Theme-safe: use a warning/negative accent for the falling log-prob lines and the leaking-mass bucket, positive accent for the deceptively-healthy accuracy panel; both themes legible. All curves are illustrative (shape matters, NO invented benchmark numbers beyond the 0.5 coin-flip and ~0.7 reference marks the chapter itself cites). Keep everything inside the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: dpo-variant-family-map
new_figures.concept: DPO and its descendants are a small set of orthogonal design choices: reward read-off, loss shape, reference model yes/no, paired data yes/no.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter explicitly frames 'A unified view' -- almost every method is a choice of (a) reward, (b) loss shape, (c) reference anchor -- and provides a table plus a decision tree. A visual map centered on DPO with each variant shown as 'DPO minus/changing ONE thing' turns the six-method zoo into a single mental model, which is exactly the organizing intuition an interviewer probes. The table is good but linear; the figure gives the family its geometry.
new_figures.brief: A radial / hub-and-spoke map with DPO at the center as the reference point, and five spokes to IPO, KTO, ORPO, SimPO, CPO. Center node 'DPO': tag its four attributes as small pills -- reward 'beta*log(pi/pi_ref), summed', loss 'log-sigmoid (Bradley-Terry)', reference 'YES', paired 'YES'. Each spoke node shows ONLY what changes relative to DPO, with the changed attribute highlighted and unchanged ones greyed: IPO -> loss becomes 'squared error to target 1/(2beta)', badge 'finite margin, noise-robust'; KTO -> paired becomes 'NO (thumbs up/down)', loss 'prospect-theory value vs z0', badge 'unpaired'; ORPO -> reference becomes 'NO', reward 'log-odds ratio', loss 'SFT NLL + log-sigmoid(OR)', badge 'single stage, fused with SFT'; SimPO -> reference 'NO', reward 'length-normalized (beta/|y|)*logpi + margin gamma', badge 'no length bias'; CPO -> reference 'NO', reward 'beta*logpi summed', loss 'log-sigmoid + SFT anchor', badge 'memory-light'. Encode the two big axes visually: draw a light dividing guide so nodes that DROP the reference model (ORPO, SimPO, CPO) sit on one side and nodes that KEEP it (DPO, IPO, KTO) on the other; mark KTO distinctly as the only 'unpaired' node (e.g. a different border style). Add a compact decision-tree ribbon along the bottom mirroring the chapter: 'paired + can afford reference -> DPO (beta=0.1)', 'noisy/near-deterministic labels -> IPO', 'only unpaired ratings -> KTO', 'one stage / no reference / tight memory -> ORPO or SimPO'. Theme-safe: use one accent to highlight the CHANGED attribute per node and neutral/greyed for inherited-from-DPO attributes; legible in both themes. Keep all nodes and labels inside the viewBox, no overlapping spokes, clear reading order from center outward.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich and derivation-heavy, and its two existing figures (dpo-margin-gradient-regimes, dpo-vs-ppo) are both strong keepers that already serve the self-curriculum gradient and the DPO-vs-PPO contrast in both themes. The gaps are the chapter's actual headline mechanisms, which currently live only as prose equations: (1) the reward-model-cancellation derivation where beta*logZ(x) cancels in the Bradley-Terry difference -- the single most foundational missing visual and the top interview question; (2) the closed-form Boltzmann tilt that gives the optimal policy its shape and makes beta a temperature; (3) the degeneration footgun (margin up while both log-probs crash), the chapter's second Interview Corner; and (4) a variant family map turning the six-method zoo into orthogonal design choices. Priorities 1,2,2,3 respectively.
